1. Introduction

Overview

This research aims to delve into the intricate nuances surrounding migration rates, particularly focusing on discerning disparities between developed and developing countries. Our investigation seeks to unravel the driving forces behind migration patterns within these distinct groups, exploring the potential influences of economic indicators such as GDP, demographic factors like Population Growth Rate, and the social support infrastructure. Crucially, we aim to ascertain whether there exists a causal relationship between these variables or if their associations are merely correlations.

Data Sources

The dataset utilized for this research is a compilation of global demographic, economic, social, and environmental data. Drawing from reputable sources such as the United Nations, World Bank, ‘World Happiness Report,’ and ‘World Development Indicators | Databank (GDP),’ our data wrangling process is also informed by the OECD Better Life Index. This robust dataset serves as the foundation for our in-depth analysis of migration dynamics.

Responsible Entities

The collaborative effort behind this research involves international researchers and prominent organizations such as the United Nations, World Bank, ‘World Happiness Report,’ and the OECD. The governments of the countries included in the dataset actively contribute to this collective initiative. The goal is to comprehensively understand the overarching patterns and dynamics of migration globally. Rigorous data collection and analysis form the backbone of this collaborative venture, supporting evidence-based policymaking, fostering international cooperation, and enhancing our collective knowledge to address the challenges and opportunities associated with migration on a global scale.

Special Aspects and Considerations

The dataset encompasses a myriad of factors, spanning economic, social, and environmental dimensions, ensuring a comprehensive analysis of migration rates. However, given the vastness of the dataset, comprising over 100 countries worldwide, potential biases may arise from variations in data reporting across different regions. This may impact the accuracy of specific demographic indicators. Notably, there might be missing voices, particularly from regions with limited access to reliable demographic information. Consequently, certain variables in the dataset contain missing values (NA), prompting the need to filter specific exemplary groups of nations without NA variables to represent our data wrangling process. These considerations underline the complexity of the dataset and the conscientious approach taken in its analysis.

2. Data Explanation and Exploration

a. Data Wrangling

First, I made 2 separate data sets. As solely using GDP would be insufficient, I thought of using the OECD Better Life Index would be perfect to make create 2 data set: one data set of 6 developed nations and one of 6 developing nations. Then, I created a small data set called “developed_nations” that consists of countries that are placed in the top 18 of the OECD Better Life Index. The second data set, we named “developed_nations” that has countries not in the top 40 of the OECD Better Life Index. The first group consists of the US, the UK, Switzerland, Germany, and France, and Australia and the second group consists of Guinea, Ethiopia, Tanzania, Liberia, Iran, and Uganda.

b. Comparing Migration Rates between Developed Nations and Developing Nations

Firstly, I compare the migration rates between my two groups of nations.Examining the graph below, we can see that all of the developing countries tend to exhibit lower migration rates, while all of the developed countries show higher migration rates. There is a subtle correlation inferred from the net migration formula (Net Migration= Immigrants - Emigrants), suggesting that developing countries tend to have more emigrants, whereas more developed countries have more immigrants.

c. Comparing Migration Rates and Population Growth Rate for Developed Nations

Next, I generate linear regression analysis for Migration.Rate and Population.Growth.Rate variables in the developed_nations dataset. As we can see from the results, the coefficient for Population.Growth.Rate is statistically significant (p-value = 0.00859, which is less than the normal significant level of 0.05), indicating that there is a significant linear relationship between Migration.Rate and Population.Growth.Rate.The positive coefficient (5.5746) suggests that as the Population.Growth.Rate increases, the Migration.Rate tends to increase as well. Moreover, the high R-squared value (0.8525) indicates that the model explains a substantial portion of the variability in Migration.Rate.To sum up, the results suggest that Population.Growth.Rate is a significant predictor of Migration.Rate in the developed_nations dataset. The linear equation for the relationship between Migration Rate (Y) and Population Growth Rate (X) based on the given regression model is Migration.Rate=−0.2510+5.5746×Population.Growth.Rate.

## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = Migration.Rate ~ Population.Growth.Rate, data = developed_nations)
## 
## Residuals:
##        1        2        3        4        5        6 
## -0.06383 -1.05607  0.54333  1.03304 -0.21928 -0.23719 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                        Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)   
## (Intercept)             -0.2510     0.7866  -0.319  0.76561   
## Population.Growth.Rate   5.5746     1.1592   4.809  0.00859 **
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.8041 on 4 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.8525, Adjusted R-squared:  0.8157 
## F-statistic: 23.13 on 1 and 4 DF,  p-value: 0.008591

d. Comparing Migration Rates and Population for Developing Nations

There is an inverted parabola pattern in the scatter plot that I generated of Migration Rate against Population in the developing_nations dataset. This suggests a nonlinear relationship between the two variables, which is not a clear correlation. The inverted U-shape may imply that there is an optimal range of population sizes for higher migration rates. The developing nations with populations within the range of 0.07 to 0.54 experience higher migration rates, while those with extremely high or low migration rates may have lower population. The U-shaped pattern may reflect complex dynamics in migration. For instance, large populations might attract migrants initially, but factors such as social issues may lead to decreased migration rates at extremely high population levels.

3. Statistical Analysis and Interpretation

a. Social Support vs Migration Rate

As we can see, migration rates are higher in developed countries. Developed countries have higher social support scores, with four countries holding the highest scores. Developing countries show a more clustered distribution of social support scores, with most falling within a middle range. There are also two developing countries with the lowest social support scores.The higher migration rates in developed countries, coupled with a diverse range of social support scores, suggest a nuanced relationship between social support and migration. It could imply that social support alone may not be the sole determinant of migration rates, and other factors, such as GDP may also play significant roles.

b. GDP vs Migration Rate

i. Visualization

From the visualization, we can see that the smoothed line for developing nations has a subtle straight-line pattern with a small negative slope. It suggests a relatively weak negative correlation between GDP per Capita and Migration Rate. As GDP per Capita increases, the Migration Rate tends to decrease slightly. The larger range of GDP per Capita (from near 0 to 9500) indicates a more varied relationship. While some developing nations with lower GDP per Capita may have higher Migration Rates, the relationship is not as consistent.The positive slope of the smoothed line indicates a positive correlation between GDP per Capita and Migration Rates in developed nations. The limited range of GDP per Capita (from 10750 to 11250) suggests that developed nations with higher GDP per Capita tend to have higher Migration Rates.

## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

ii. Pearson Test

To further proof the strong correlation between GDP per capita and the Migration Rate in developed countries, I run a Pearson test for this two variables. As we can see, the correlation coefficient is 0.4732, indicating a good positive correlation between Migration Rate and Logged GDP per Capita for developed nations. The p-value is 0.3432, which is greater than significance level of 0.05. Therefore, we should not reject the null hypothesis that the correlation is equal to zero.

## 
##  Pearson's product-moment correlation
## 
## data:  developed_nations$Migration.Rate and developed_nations$Logged.GDP.per.capita
## t = 1.0742, df = 4, p-value = 0.3432
## alternative hypothesis: true correlation is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  -0.5493427  0.9282696
## sample estimates:
##       cor 
## 0.4731695

c. Death Rate vs Migration Rate

As we can see, the dots for developed nations exhibit a large negative slope trend, indicating a strong negative correlation between Death Rate and Migration Rate for the developed nations. This means that higher death rates are associated with higher migration rates in developed countries. In contrast, the dots for developing nations follow a small positive slope trend, indicating a weak positive correlation between Death Rate and Migration Rate for this group of nations. Therefore, there’s a less pronounced relationship between death rates and migration rates in these countries.

d. Pearson Test for Migration Rates and Healthy Life Expectancy in Developing Nations

As we can see, the correlation coefficient is 0.4892, indicating a moderate positive correlation between Healthy Life Expectancy and Migration Rate for developing nations. Moreover,the p-value is 0.403, which is greater than the significance level of 0.05, indicating that we should not reject the null hypothesis that the correlation is equal to zero.

## 
##  Pearson's product-moment correlation
## 
## data:  developing_nations$Healthy.life.expectancy and developing_nations$Migration.Rate
## t = 0.64089, df = 4, p-value = 0.5565
## alternative hypothesis: true correlation is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  -0.6730981  0.8950554
## sample estimates:
##     cor 
## 0.30516

4. Conclusion

Our data exploration and statistical analyses unveiled nuanced relationships between migration rates and various factors.These findings emphasize the need for a comprehensive understanding of the interplay of various factors in shaping migration patterns. The influence of Population Growth Rate on Migration Rate in developed nations emerged as a significant predictor. In addition, population does not have a clear linear relationship with migration rates for developing nations. For both nation groups, the relationships between social support and migration rates is not significant. Moreover, the exploration of GDP, death rates, and healthy life expectancy in relation to migration rates provided valuable insights. The complex relationships observed underscore the multifaceted nature of migration dynamics, with factors intertwining in intricate ways. While GDP per capita and death rates show weak correlations with migration rates in developing nations, these associations are more pronounced in developed nations. On the other hand, healthy life expectancy exhibits a strong positive correlation with migration rates in developing countries. In summary, this research contributes to the broader discourse on migration, offering insights that can inform policymaking and international cooperation. The complexities uncovered highlight the importance of continued research in this field to address the challenges and opportunities associated with migration on a global scale.

5. Reference List

OECD. (2018). Better Life Index. OECD.Stat. https://stats.oecd.org/Index.aspx?DataSetCode=BLI

World Development Indicators | DataBank (GDP). (n.d.). Retrieved December 14, 2023, from https://databank.worldbank.org/reports.aspx?source=2&series=NY.GDP.MKTP.CD&country

World Happiness Report 2021. (n.d.). Singh, Ajaypal. “World Happiness Report 2021.” Kaggle.com, 2021. https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/ajaypalsinghlo/world-happiness-report-2021?select=world-happiness-report-2021.csv.

World Population Analysis. (2023, October 5). https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/parasharmanas/world-population-analysis
